## engineering.architecture.better_version_now_future_proof_by_default - Better Version Now By Default

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `architecture_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `architecture`, `coding`, `clean-code`, `ddd-adiv`, `future-first`, `better-version-now`, `borrowed-before-invented`, `contracts`, `boundaries`, `data-ownership`, `security`, `migrations`, `performance`, `verification`, `profile-fit`, `legacy`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Programming tasks should check whether a small profile-fit, research-informed improvement should be done now across clean code, DDD/ADIV, contracts, security, data ownership, migrations, diagnostics, verification, and predictable performance.

For every implementation, bugfix, migration, refactor, performance diagnosis, code review, and architecture decision, ask whether a modest better-version-now step should be included. For non-obvious source-code writes, first run the web research spike gate unless the active plan already names the mechanism. Default to yes when a small step cheaply improves ownership, readability, security, data boundaries, contracts, migration safety, observability, verification, or future change cost. Apply project-profile gating: legacy/sunset/custom-framework surgical changes stay surgical and match surrounding code; broad boundaries, anti-corruption layers, and paradigm migration require an explicit refactor, modernization, migration, or broad feature task. Borrow known solutions and proven patterns before inventing custom systems. If the better version is substantially larger, speculative, ceremony-heavy, or unclear, stop for an overengineering check.

Rationale: The rule prevents known expensive refactors without turning every ticket into architecture work. Future-proofing is valuable when small and profile-fit; it is harmful when it ignores legacy constraints, test reality, or task size.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Shared agent contract defines the global better-version-now and borrowed-before-invented directive.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code pack applies the directive to programming implementation defaults.
- `project/code/WORKFLOW.json` `general_workflow` duplicate - Code workflow makes the check part of every programming task sequence.
- `core/PROJECT-PROFILE.json` `behavior_impact` duplicate - Project profile gates how strongly better-version-now and DDD-ADIV apply to legacy, greenfield, custom-framework, and no-test contexts.
name: engineering.architecture.better_version_now_future_proof_by_default
summary: Programming tasks should check whether a small profile-fit, research-informed improvement should be done now across clean code, DDD/ADIV, contracts, security, data ownership, migrations, diagnostics, verification, and predictable performance.
title: Better Version Now By Default

## engineering.architecture.helper_enforced_contracts - Helpers Enforce Contracts By Convenience

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `should`
- subtype: `architecture_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `architecture`, `source-of-truth`, `contracts`, `helpers`, `diagnostics`, `ddd-adiv`, `tooling`, `legacy`, `process-design`, `habits`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Prefer shared helpers, APIs, registries, schemas, or small service boundaries as contract aids when they make correct usage the convenient/default path and off-contract usage obvious.

When multiple call sites must follow the same technical or domain contract, look for a small shared helper, API, registry, schema, adapter, or service boundary that carries the contract by default. Treat the helper as an aid to the contract and architecture, not as a shortcut around ownership, domain boundaries, or framework intent. Prefer this when it improves ownership, diagnostics, verification, and reuse across projects. In legacy or surgical zones, helper contracts are often the right enforcement shape because they make the safe path convenient without forcing a paradigm migration. Do not force the pattern when it would hide hot-path cost, blur domain ownership, add ceremony to a one-off behavior, or fight an established framework boundary.

Rationale: A convenient shared helper turns architectural discipline into the path of least resistance, reduces duplicated local interpretations, and gives agents and developers one auditable place to debug or evolve the rule. This is the practical form of convenience drives habit: durable correctness should not require repeated heroic discipline.

Sources:
- `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `tagged_rules` - Reusable source-pipeline rule promoted from child-project logger/session and movement resolver work.
name: engineering.architecture.helper_enforced_contracts
summary: Prefer shared helpers, APIs, registries, schemas, or small service boundaries as contract aids when they make correct usage the convenient/default path and off-contract usage obvious.
title: Helpers Enforce Contracts By Convenience

## engineering.coding.ai_native_code_contract_headers - AI-Native Code Contract Headers

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `should`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `engineering`, `coding`, `comments`, `ai-native`, `ownership`, `contracts`, `diagnostics`, `layer-purity`, `maintainability`, `human-ai-hybrid`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Major source files may carry a short top-of-file JSON-like comment block that states ownership, non-goals, diagnostics, and layer boundaries for human/AI/hybrid maintenance.

Use an `AI_CONTRACT` header only for major systems, shared boundaries, runtime/performance/diagnostic modules, layer seams, and files repeatedly touched by humans and agents. Place it at the beginning of the file as a block of comment-prefixed JSON-like fields: purpose, owns, must_not, design_goals, non_goals, diagnostics.taxonomy, diagnostics.fields, layer.name, layer.cannot_depend_on, and ai_notes. Keep it short and factual. Do not add boilerplate to tiny leaf files, generated files, third-party/vendor files, obvious DTO/config-only files, or code where names and types already carry the full meaning. Treat the header as a reading and audit contract, not a substitute for clear names, tests, schemas, runtime checks, or actual dependency enforcement. Use `tools/codebase/ai_native_contract_dry_run.py` to inspect candidate rollout files before adding headers; broad header rollout should happen through explicit per-system passes.

Rationale: The user's code will be maintained by a human/AI/hybrid process. A small machine-readable comment contract helps future agents find ownership, avoid forbidden responsibilities, preserve diagnostic taxonomy, and respect layer purity without turning every file into stale prose.

Sources:
- `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `tagged_rules` - Reusable code contract standard for AI-assisted maintenance.
- `tools/codebase/ai_native_contract_dry_run.py` `script` duplicate - Dry-run helper that prints the standard and candidate rollout files without editing source.
- `profiles/SocratexGamedev/WORKFLOW.json` `contextual_actions` duplicate - Gamedev profile adds FPS/runtime/diagnostic/layer-purity lens for game-system headers.
name: engineering.coding.ai_native_code_contract_headers
summary: Major source files may carry a short top-of-file JSON-like comment block that states ownership, non-goals, diagnostics, and layer boundaries for human/AI/hybrid maintenance.
title: AI-Native Code Contract Headers

## engineering.coding.borrowed_before_invented - Borrowed Before Invented

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `architecture`, `best-practices`, `borrowed-before-invented`, `coding`, `domain_modeling`, `engineering`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Prefer established patterns, proven libraries, and standard tools over custom-built solutions when they fit the project profile.

Before designing custom code, check whether an established pattern, archetype, standard workflow, library, or proven tool already solves the problem at lower cost. For non-obvious write-code work, use the web research spike gate to find current archetypes and known failure modes before choosing a invented design. Apply the borrow-vs-invent lens to: design patterns (state machines, queues, adapters, ports, repositories), platform conventions, schemas, transaction boundaries, and contract types. Only invent when the borrowed option creates worse constraints, hides project-specific truth, or fails the profile-fit check. Local patterns shape the implementation after research; they do not automatically overrule research. Document the decision when an invented solution is chosen.

Rationale: Custom code carries hidden ownership and maintenance cost. Borrowing reduces initial complexity and surfaces failure modes that are already documented; invented solutions should pay for themselves with a concrete project-specific advantage.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Source-of-truth for the borrowed-before-invented directive.
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `known_solution_bias` duplicate - Domain-specific expansion of the same directive.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `known_solutions_alignment` duplicate - DDD-ADIV reinforces borrow-vs-invent as a domain-clarifying tool.
name: engineering.coding.borrowed_before_invented
summary: Prefer established patterns, proven libraries, and standard tools over custom-built solutions when they fit the project profile.
title: Borrowed Before Invented

## engineering.coding.business_naming - Business Naming with Technical Suffix

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `best-practices`, `coding`, `ddd-adiv`, `domain_modeling`, `engineering`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Names should reflect business intent, not implementation mechanism. Reading the code should explain WHAT the system does in domain terms; technical suffixes carry the HOW.

When naming classes, methods, variables, files, and database columns: (1) The base name expresses business meaning (Quote, Customer, OverduePayment, EligibilityCheck, ClawbackCalculation) - not generic mechanism nouns (Manager, Handler, Processor, Helper, Util). (2) Technical role goes in the SUFFIX, not the base name: QuoteRepository, OverduePaymentNotifier, EligibilityCheckResult, ClawbackReportBuilder. (3) Avoid abbreviations unless they are project-domain canon (CRM, GDPR, MLE if everyone on the project knows them). (4) Method names describe the business action: markQuoteAsAccepted(), recalculateClawbackForCancelledOrder() - not processItem() or doStuff(). (5) Boolean variables and methods read as predicates: isOverdue(), hasUnresolvedClawback(), customerCanReceiveRefund. (6) Variables hold the WHY in their name: $eligibleCustomers (not $tmp), $unsentReminders (not $arr2). When the project has an existing domain glossary, prefer those exact terms even when shorter neutral names would technically work.

Rationale: Code is read more often than written, and the dominant reading pattern is 'what is this system doing in business terms?' - not 'what mechanism is being used?'. Business-named code answers the dominant question first; technical suffixes add the secondary information without obscuring intent.

Sources:
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `domain_driven` duplicate - DDD domain-driven dimension: business-meaningful names over mechanism names, intent-revealing methods.
name: engineering.coding.business_naming
summary: Names should reflect business intent, not implementation mechanism. Reading the code should explain WHAT the system does in domain terms; technical suffixes carry the HOW.
title: Business Naming with Technical Suffix

## engineering.coding.code_primary_source_of_truth - Code Is The Primary Source Of Truth

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `coding`, `naming`, `comments`, `ddd-adiv`, `maintainability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Names, files, methods, variables, and parameters should carry business meaning directly; comments are side notes only when code cannot encode the intent or warning well enough.

Treat code as the primary source of truth. File names should suggest how methods are interpreted; method names should suggest how variables and parameters are interpreted. Names should be obvious enough to derive business meaning from the code. Use comments only for missing typing/context, reader warnings, workarounds, hacks, non-obvious implementation constraints, or business intent/specification that code cannot express. Do not write comments that merely describe what the code already says.

Rationale: Long-running agent work is cheaper and safer when meaning is encoded in names and ownership boundaries instead of duplicated prose that can drift.

Sources:
- `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `tagged_rules` - Durable coding discipline promoted from child-project pipeline work.
name: engineering.coding.code_primary_source_of_truth
summary: Names, files, methods, variables, and parameters should carry business meaning directly; comments are side notes only when code cannot encode the intent or warning well enough.
title: Code Is The Primary Source Of Truth

## engineering.coding.codebase_as_contract - Codebase Is the Contract — Make It as Strict as Possible

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `should`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `architecture`, `best-practices`, `coding`, `domain_modeling`, `engineering`, `future-first`, `production-grade`, `verification`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Treat the code itself (types, schemas, signatures, validation, structure) as the primary contract. Make it as machine-checkable as the language and project allow.

When writing or modifying code, choose the strictest valid representation: (1) types over untyped parameters when the language version supports them; (2) value objects over primitive obsession (Money vs float, EmailAddress vs string); (3) schema validation at boundaries (DTOs, JSON schemas, parsers - not ad-hoc isset chains); (4) explicit enums or named constants over magic strings/numbers; (5) named function signatures over loose array bags; (6) immutable-by-default where the language supports it. The language target may forbid some of these (e.g., PHP 5.6 has no typed properties) - use what IS available and match the project profile. The point: a future maintainer should be able to learn what is true about the system by reading the code, not by reading commit messages or wikis. Documentation rots; the code is what runs.

Rationale: Documentation drifts; tests cover specific cases; contracts in the language itself stay aligned with the runtime. The stricter the code-as-contract, the less the system depends on tribal knowledge or external documentation that goes stale.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `operating_principles` duplicate - Operating principles include 'prefer explicit contracts over hidden convention'.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `invariant_visible` duplicate - Invariant-visible dimension: validation at boundaries, explicit factories, schema checks.
name: engineering.coding.codebase_as_contract
summary: Treat the code itself (types, schemas, signatures, validation, structure) as the primary contract. Make it as machine-checkable as the language and project allow.
title: Codebase Is the Contract — Make It as Strict as Possible

## engineering.coding.ddd_adiv_when_possible - DDD-ADIV When Possible

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `should`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `architecture`, `coding`, `ddd-adiv`, `domain_modeling`, `engineering`, `ownership`, `verification`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Use DDD-ADIV (Domain-Driven, Architecture-Decisive, Invariant-Visible, Verification-Attached) as a practical design bias when it reduces ambiguity, future retrofit cost, or hidden coupling - without turning it into ceremony.

Before substantive code design, ask the four questions: (1) Domain language - does the name reflect business meaning rather than mechanism? (2) Architecture-decisive - are ownership and dependency direction explicit? (3) Invariant-visible - are important rules expressed as checks/schemas/tests/validations? (4) Verification-attached - is the risky behavior testable or at least observable? In non-legacy, greenfield, early, or actively modernized zones, use DDD-ADIV as the default design shape. When project profile is `legacy` or per-zone config sets `ddd_adiv: false|soft`, treat DDD-ADIV as a lens-not-mandate: use helper contracts, adapters, seams, diagnostics, or small isolated files to make the safe behavior easy, and pull full DDD ownership only when rewriting heavily or explicitly modernizing. Read `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` for the full definition. Do not turn it into ceremony - skip when the change is genuinely shallow.

Rationale: DDD-ADIV is the workspace's recommended default because explicit boundaries and named invariants survive long-running maintenance better than clever or convenient code. Per-zone overrides exist so the lens does not force-fit legacy code; legacy usually needs enforceable helper contracts before it can support broader domain boundaries.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `ddd_adiv_default` - Shared contract definition of DDD-ADIV bias.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `domain_driven` duplicate - Full code-pack DDD-ADIV definition.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `architecture_decisive` duplicate - Architecture-decisive dimension.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `invariant_visible` duplicate - Invariant-visible dimension.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `verification_attached` duplicate - Verification-attached dimension.
name: engineering.coding.ddd_adiv_when_possible
summary: Use DDD-ADIV (Domain-Driven, Architecture-Decisive, Invariant-Visible, Verification-Attached) as a practical design bias when it reduces ambiguity, future retrofit cost, or hidden coupling - without turning it into ceremony.
title: DDD-ADIV When Possible

## engineering.coding.legacy_aware_change_split - Legacy-Aware Change Split — Surgical or Extract-to-Clean-File

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `architecture`, `best-practices`, `coding`, `engineering`, `ownership`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

In legacy zones (project profile = legacy or per-zone config says 'do not modernize'), make changes surgical. For non-trivial changes, extract the new logic to a separate clean file rather than scattering large diffs across legacy code — '499 deleted lines + 2 clean new files', not '500 changed lines'.

Before modifying legacy code, classify the change. (A) Surgical fix: small, follows surrounding patterns - edit in place, do not modernize, match existing style even if older, and prefer a small helper/adapter/diagnostic/check when a repeated contract needs enforcement. (B) Non-trivial change: would touch many lines or introduce concepts foreign to that zone - DO NOT scatter the changes across the legacy file or force a DDD paradigm migration into it. Instead extract the new logic to a separate clean file (in the modern zone if one exists, or as a small dedicated legacy file with clean structure), then make the legacy site call into it with the smallest possible delta. The diff should read as 'small change at legacy call site + a new readable file', not 'sprawling rewrite mid-legacy-file'. (C) Heavy rewrite: same as (B) but the new file lives in the modern zone if the project has one and may own a proper DDD-ADIV boundary. Check `project_profile.lifecycle` and per-zone config in PIPELINE-CONFIG.json to decide which mode applies. Always favor the diff shape that keeps reviewers focused on the small call-site change while the new logic gets a clean, isolated home.

Rationale: Mixing modern style into legacy files creates style-inversion zones that confuse both human readers and inspection tools. Extracting to a new file gives the new code a clean home, keeps the legacy file readable as legacy, and gives reviewers a clear before/after on the legacy call site without losing the new logic in diff noise. Helper contracts are the durable legacy enforcement path; full DDD-ADIV belongs where ownership can actually be maintained.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` duplicate - Bug-fix directive: prefer fix that removes class of bug or improves diagnosis without over-expanding scope.
name: engineering.coding.legacy_aware_change_split
summary: In legacy zones (project profile = legacy or per-zone config says 'do not modernize'), make changes surgical. For non-trivial changes, extract the new logic to a separate clean file rather than scattering large diffs across legacy code — '499 deleted lines + 2 clean new files', not '500 changed lines'.
title: Legacy-Aware Change Split — Surgical or Extract-to-Clean-File

## engineering.coding.legacy_helpers_nonlegacy_ddd_adiv - Legacy Uses Helper Contracts, Non-Legacy Uses DDD-ADIV

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `architecture`, `coding`, `ddd-adiv`, `legacy`, `contracts`, `helpers`, `ownership`, `verification`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

In legacy zones, enforce behavior through small helpers, adapters, seams, diagnostics, or interface contracts; in non-legacy work, use DDD-ADIV boundaries as the default design shape.

Before shaping a code change, classify the zone. In non-legacy, greenfield, early, or actively modernized code, use DDD-ADIV as the default: name the domain concept, make ownership and dependency direction explicit, expose invariants, and attach verification. In legacy or surgical zones, do not force a DDD paradigm migration into the old shape. Instead add the smallest helper, adapter, seam, schema, diagnostic, or interface contract that makes correct usage easy and off-contract usage visible while preserving the surrounding style. Use a broader DDD-ADIV boundary only when the task explicitly includes modernization, extraction, or a broad feature surface where the new boundary can own the behavior cleanly.

Rationale: Forcing DDD purity onto legacy often creates wrong-shape architecture debt: modern concepts scattered inside code that cannot support them. Helper contracts respect the boundary that can actually be maintained; DDD-ADIV remains the preferred shape where ownership can be made real.

Sources:
- `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `engineering.architecture.helper_enforced_contracts` - Helper contracts are the legacy-compatible enforcement path.
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `domain_driven` duplicate - Non-legacy default still follows the full DDD-ADIV definition.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `profile_aware_defaults` duplicate - Profile-aware defaults decide whether the task is legacy/surgical or non-legacy/modernizable.
name: engineering.coding.legacy_helpers_nonlegacy_ddd_adiv
summary: In legacy zones, enforce behavior through small helpers, adapters, seams, diagnostics, or interface contracts; in non-legacy work, use DDD-ADIV boundaries as the default design shape.
title: Legacy Uses Helper Contracts, Non-Legacy Uses DDD-ADIV

## engineering.coding.match_surrounding_patterns - Match Surrounding Patterns Mid-Fix

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `best-practices`, `coding`, `engineering`, `ownership`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

When making non-rewrite changes, match the conventions of the surrounding file/zone — even if those conventions are older or weaker. Do not modernize mid-fix.

When editing existing code that you do not own end-to-end: (1) Read the surrounding file and the immediate neighbors in that directory before writing. (2) Match the existing style (naming, indentation, error handling, structural patterns) even if it is older or you would do it differently in greenfield code. (3) If the surrounding code uses a deprecated or weak pattern, leave it alone for THIS change - a separate refactor task can address it. (4) Cross-zone moves (e.g., legacy → modern) are a separate decision per legacy_aware_change_split; do not casually drag legacy patterns into modern zones or modern patterns into legacy zones as a side-effect of a feature. The exception: when the per-project profile or zone config explicitly authorizes modernization in this zone (e.g., bias-true DDD-ADIV in a modern zone), the new code follows the modern conventions even if the file is partially legacy - but the diff should still avoid scattering migration churn across unrelated lines.

Rationale: Style inversions inside legacy files create cognitive overhead for every future reader. The cost of leaving an old pattern alone for one fix is small; the cost of leaving a half-modernized file is paid forever. Match-surrounding is the cheapest way to keep diffs reviewable.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `operating_principles` duplicate - Operating principles emphasize smallest meaningful ownership slice.
name: engineering.coding.match_surrounding_patterns
summary: When making non-rewrite changes, match the conventions of the surrounding file/zone — even if those conventions are older or weaker. Do not modernize mid-fix.
title: Match Surrounding Patterns Mid-Fix

## engineering.coding.method_and_variable_extraction - Extract Methods and Intent-Revealing Variables

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `best-practices`, `coding`, `diagnostics`, `domain_modeling`, `engineering`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Pull repeated logic into named methods. Extract intermediate values into intent-revealing variables. Keep functions short and single-purpose.

When code starts doing multiple things, extract: (1) repeated expressions into intent-revealing local variables (e.g. eligibleCustomers = customers.filter(...)) instead of inline complex expressions; (2) multi-step blocks into private methods named after their business intent (sendOverdueReminders rather than processList); (3) deeply nested conditionals into guard-clause helper methods. Targets: each function should fit on a screen and operate at one level of abstraction; named locals should explain WHY a value is what it is, not just hold it. Do not extract for the sake of file count - extract when naming the extraction makes the call site read more like business logic.

Rationale: Inline cleverness compresses the WHY into mental load. Named extractions move the WHY into the name itself, so the call site reads like documentation. This is the single highest-ROI readability lever in legacy code.

Sources:
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code-pack defaults already mention small functions and intent-revealing helpers.
name: engineering.coding.method_and_variable_extraction
summary: Pull repeated logic into named methods. Extract intermediate values into intent-revealing variables. Keep functions short and single-purpose.
title: Extract Methods and Intent-Revealing Variables

## engineering.coding.minimal_comments - Minimal Comments — Workarounds and Business Intent Only

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `best-practices`, `coding`, `engineering`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Default to no comments. Add a comment only when it explains a non-obvious WHY: a workaround for a specific bug, a business invariant, a hidden constraint, or surprising behavior. Never restate WHAT the code does.

Comment policy: (1) NEVER restate visible code - well-named identifiers and intent-revealing methods already do that. (2) NEVER reference the current task, PR number, or ticket inside code - those belong in the commit message and PR description (they rot in code). (3) DO comment when the WHY is non-obvious: 'we round here because the upstream system returns 0.999...', 'workaround for issue #X in library Y' (only library bug references stay), 'this branch handles the case where billing flips between cycles'. (4) DO comment business intent that the code shape cannot capture: 'overdue means 30+ days for retail customers, 60+ for enterprise (per agreement clause 4.2)'. (5) Multi-paragraph docblocks are forbidden unless the language requires them for type generation; one short line max for non-obvious WHY. (6) TODO/FIXME for follow-ups is allowed when the alternative would silently leave the issue uncaptured.

Rationale: Comments rot independently of the code they describe. The only comments that survive over time are the ones that capture knowledge the code itself cannot: workarounds, business rules, hidden invariants. Everything else creates noise and false security as it drifts out of sync.

Sources:
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code-pack defaults: 'Comments should explain non-obvious constraints, workarounds, hazards, or tradeoffs; do not restate visible code.'
name: engineering.coding.minimal_comments
summary: Default to no comments. Add a comment only when it explains a non-obvious WHY: a workaround for a specific bug, a business invariant, a hidden constraint, or surprising behavior. Never restate WHAT the code does.
title: Minimal Comments — Workarounds and Business Intent Only

## engineering.coding.production_grade_default - Production-Grade Architectural Default

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `should`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `architecture`, `best-practices`, `coding`, `data-first`, `diagnostics`, `engineering`, `future-first`, `ownership`, `performance`, `production-grade`, `verification`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

For programming feature requests, bias toward production-grade architecture: explicit ownership, data flow, contracts, toolability, diagnostics, performance budget, deterministic behavior, testability, low retrofit cost.

When implementing features in software/games, evaluate the requested approach against a production-grade lens before executing. Required dimensions: explicit ownership of state and side effects; explicit data flow (no hidden pipelines); machine-readable contracts (types, schemas, JSON shapes); toolability (linters, scripts, tests can run on the artifact); diagnostics (the change can be traced and verified mechanically); performance budget (no silent O(n^2) where O(n) was reasonable); deterministic behavior where relevant; testability (verification is attachable); low retrofit cost (future modifications are not blocked). If the user's path is weaker than a better future-facing sequence, propose the better sequence before executing.

Rationale: Production-grade defaults reduce future retrofit cost and make code mechanically diagnosable. The cost of doing it badly first is paid by every future maintainer, so the bias should be toward strong defaults unless the project profile (e.g., throwaway prototype) explicitly relaxes them.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Production-grade directive for code feature requests.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code-pack technical defaults reinforce the same architectural lens.
name: engineering.coding.production_grade_default
summary: For programming feature requests, bias toward production-grade architecture: explicit ownership, data flow, contracts, toolability, diagnostics, performance budget, deterministic behavior, testability, low retrofit cost.
title: Production-Grade Architectural Default

## engineering.coding.readability_first - Readability First

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `coding_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `2`
- tags: `best-practices`, `coding`, `diagnostics`, `engineering`, `readability`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Prefer readable, diagnosable code over compact or clever code. Code is read more often than written; future maintainers - including future-you and AI agents - pay the cost of cleverness.

When writing or modifying code, optimize for readability first: explicit control flow over clever tricks; explicit data flow over hidden pipelines; guard clauses over deep nesting; small functions over multi-purpose blocks; intent-revealing names over abbreviated ones; one block focused on one concern at one abstraction level; structured APIs/parsers over ad-hoc string manipulation. Comments explain non-obvious WHY (constraints, hazards, tradeoffs) - not WHAT. Top-down code organization when practical: entry points and high-level workflow first, helpers later. If a clever optimization is required, attach a short comment explaining why and a verification (test/benchmark) for the gain.

Rationale: SocratexPipeline is built on the assumption that long-running diagnosability matters more than micro-cleverness. Code that is hard to read becomes hard to diagnose, hard to modify safely, and hard for AI agents to reason about - compounding maintenance cost over time.

Sources:
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` - Code-pack technical defaults already mandate readability - this entry surfaces it as a tagged rule for the knowledge layer.
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `operating_principles` duplicate - Operating principles include 'prefer explicit contracts over hidden convention'.
name: engineering.coding.readability_first
summary: Prefer readable, diagnosable code over compact or clever code. Code is read more often than written; future maintainers - including future-you and AI agents - pay the cost of cleverness.
title: Readability First

## engineering.performance.lightweight_by_default_for_predictable_load - Lightweight Futureproof For Predictable Load

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `architecture_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `performance`, `architecture`, `future-first`, `data-first`, `runtime`, `batching`, `pooling`, `hot-path`, `lightweight-by-default`, `profile-fit`, `lightweight-futureproof`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Predictable batch, hot-path, storage, I/O, event, count-heavy, dynamic, or generated-content load should use an appropriate lightweight ownership path before convenient per-object framework usage becomes the broad model.

For every implementation, bugfix, performance diagnosis, log review, and architecture decision, check whether the touched behavior is likely to become batch, hot-path, storage-heavy, I/O-heavy, event-heavy, generated-content-heavy, count-heavy, dynamic, modular, branched, or repeatedly exercised. When scale/dynamics are unclear and architecture depends on them, ask concise future-load questions before web research. When predictable load exists, prefer the profile-appropriate lightweight path: bulk/query access, explicit indexes, bounded caches, queues/background jobs, project-owned helper/service APIs, batching, pooling, activation, or specialized local queries. Use convenient framework primitives for local entity work and ordinary small operations; do not make per-object convenience the broad storage, update, runtime, or batch model for predictable scale. For performance complaints, first ask whether the real fix is migrating away from an unsuitable broad model before tuning thresholds, cache sizes, or local micro-costs.

Rationale: This is a generic lightweight futureproof heuristic. In business systems it can mean ORM for single-entity work and raw/bulk query helpers for true batch paths; in realtime profiles it can mean avoiding thousands of inactive engine objects. Profile-fit still applies: do not replace simple framework code in a small legacy ticket unless scale or measurements justify it.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Shared programming directive now includes predictable-load checks and heavyweight-model migration bias.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code pack technical defaults require lightweight-by-default contracts where load is predictable.
- `project/code/WORKFLOW.json` `general_workflow` duplicate - Code workflow applies the check during feature, bug, performance, and architecture work.
- `templates/code/context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `tagged_rules` duplicate - Starter code template carries the rule into new child projects.
name: engineering.performance.lightweight_by_default_for_predictable_load
summary: Predictable batch, hot-path, storage, I/O, event, count-heavy, dynamic, or generated-content load should use an appropriate lightweight ownership path before convenient per-object framework usage becomes the broad model.
title: Lightweight Futureproof For Predictable Load

## engineering.workflow.convenience_drives_habit - Convenience Drives Habit

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`

- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Durable engineering habits should be carried by defaults, helpers, tools, checks, or UI/process affordances, not by repeated willpower.

When proposing a coding convention, process step, review rule, UX behavior, or team habit, make the correct path the convenient/default path whenever practical. Prefer helpers, scripts, templates, schemas, lint/check gates, presets, generated files, or UI affordances that make the desired behavior easy and off-contract behavior visible. Treat willpower-only discipline as non-durable: if a technique requires conscious discipline every time it is used, assume it will not survive six months of normal work without burnout unless it is converted into a convenient default, checklist gate, automation, or small contract-carrying interface.

Rationale: Engineering culture often overvalues heroic discipline. Habits scale when the environment makes the right action cheap, visible, and repeatable; they decay when every repetition requires renewed attention.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Shared directive asks agents to prefer durable defaults, helpers, checks, and tooling over willpower-only process.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `technical_defaults` duplicate - Code pack carries this rule into programming work.
name: engineering.workflow.convenience_drives_habit
summary: Durable engineering habits should be carried by defaults, helpers, tools, checks, or UI/process affordances, not by repeated willpower.
title: Convenience Drives Habit

Tool invocation failures should default to hardening the owning script or contract, not repeating manual recovery.

When an owned repository script or tool fails because an input was easy to pass incorrectly, quoting/encoding/path handling was fragile, validation was missing, output was unclear, or a process failure is likely to recur, default to fixing or extending the owning script, wrapper, help/validation, or SCRIPTS.json input/output contract before retrying or immediately after unblocking the task. Treat manual recovery as temporary; keep it only when the failure is clearly a one-off typo, missing user-provided data, or the tool-hardening scope would be materially larger than the current task.

Rationale: Repeatable tool friction is pipeline debt. Encoding the fix into scripts and script contracts reduces future prompt cost and prevents agents from memorizing brittle workarounds.

Sources:

## engineering.workflow.normalized_content_hashes - Normalized Content Hashes For Compiled Knowledge

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `workflow`, `tooling`, `compiled-context`, `knowledge`, `hashing`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Compiled knowledge and instruction freshness hashes ignore mechanical text drift but preserve semantic whitespace.

Use normalized text-content hashes for compiled knowledge, file fallback tables, compiled instruction checksums, and generated instruction source manifests: remove UTF-8 BOM, fold CRLF/CR to LF, trim trailing spaces and tabs per line, and ignore excess final blank lines. Do not use file modification timestamps for freshness, and do not strip all whitespace, because those approaches respectively create false invalidations and hide semantic changes in JSON, Python, legacy shell, and other whitespace-sensitive formats.

Rationale: Compiled outputs should rebuild when source meaning changes, not when editor or line-ending cleanup changes; preserving indentation and interior whitespace keeps the hash safe for whitespace-sensitive files.

Sources:
- `tools/knowledge/knowledge_index.py` `normalized_hash_text` - Knowledge SQLite and file fallback source freshness use the normalized hash policy.
- `tools/pipeline/rebuild_ai_compiled_context.py` `ConvertTo-NormalizedHashText` duplicate - Compiled instruction source manifests and checksums use the same normalized hash policy.
name: engineering.workflow.normalized_content_hashes
summary: Compiled knowledge and instruction freshness hashes ignore mechanical text drift but preserve semantic whitespace.
title: Normalized Content Hashes For Compiled Knowledge

Work starts only when the goal, owner surface, scope boundary, and completion signal are clear enough to execute.

Before executing, make the task obvious enough to act on. The goal, owner surface, scope boundary, and completion signal must be clear from the user request, active plan, or loaded project state. If they are not clear, ask before starting. If new ambiguity appears while working and it can change scope, behavior, priority, or acceptance, pause and ask instead of guessing through it.

Rationale: Ambiguous execution creates hidden product decisions, wrong priorities, and expensive rework. The gate preserves momentum only when the target is concrete.

## engineering.workflow.pair_programming_collaboration - Pair-Programming Stance

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `best-practices`, `engineering`, `workflow`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Default agent stance is pair-programming colleague: investigate, suggest, trace, explain, maintain the thread of what we know — not autonomous executor and not documentation generator.

When working with the user on a code task: (1) Maintain the investigation thread - track what is known, what is unknown, what was checked, what is the next smallest verification. Do not lose state across iterations. (2) Suggest iterative next steps: when stuck, propose the smallest single thing that can be verified to make progress. (3) Spot patterns and shortcuts: 'this looks like X - have you checked Y?', 'this could be simpler if Z'. (4) Trace execution chains: when asked how something works, follow the code through dispatch / workers / callbacks / models, do not guess from names. (5) Explain mechanically when asked 'how does X work?' - give a clear, accurate, code-grounded answer. (6) Be honest about limits: 'I don't know, let me check' or 'this needs your judgment' beats fabrication. (7) Do NOT run away with implementation when the design has not been agreed; do NOT over-produce documentation artifacts; do NOT batch up multi-step rewrites without explicit approval. Decisions on priorities, architecture direction, and what ships belong to the user.

Rationale: Pair-programming with a senior engineer is the default mode in this workspace. The agent's primary value is investigative thread-keeping and pattern-spotting, not autonomous execution. Misaligning the stance produces both over-engineering and over-eager rewrites that the user has to undo.

Sources:
- `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json` `content.tagged_rules.value.30` - record source
name: engineering.workflow.pair_programming_collaboration
summary: Default agent stance is pair-programming colleague: investigate, suggest, trace, explain, maintain the thread of what we know — not autonomous executor and not documentation generator.
title: Pair-Programming Stance

Reusable scripts and script helpers should share implementation instead of duplicating logic across pipeline installs.

When two repository or pipeline scripts perform the same work or use the same function shape, resolver, parser, command runner, JSON/document operation, bootstrap/env handling, logging, validation, or output formatting, extract the behavior into a common helper/module and parameterize the callers instead of copy-pasting local implementations. Keep one canonical implementation with thin wrappers where a script-level entrypoint is still useful. For reusable SocratexPipeline behavior, prefer direct source-owned copy/sync into child projects over local rewrites; child-project scripts that must differ should isolate the project-specific delta and name why it cannot be shared. Add a final deduplication/parity pass after broad script migrations before calling the script layer complete.

Rationale: Duplicated script and helper logic creates silent behavioral drift between projects and makes future fixes expensive. A shared helper base makes the cheap path the correct path: one bugfix, one contract, one verification surface.

## engineering.workflow.tiered_context_hierarchy - Tiered Context Hierarchy

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `workflow`, `docs-workflow`, `compiled-context`, `knowledge`, `context-tier`, `context-budget`, `directive-hierarchy`
- source_of_truth: `core/CONTEXT-TIERS.json`

Keep always-loaded context small by classifying pipeline knowledge into five tiers before setting exact token budgets.

Use the tiered context hierarchy before expanding startup instructions or broad routed knowledge. Tier 1 is the small always-loaded core pipeline and decision gates. Tier 2 is routed operating guidance such as flows, main philosophies, generic rules, and task/profile rules. Tier 3 is deep patterns, archetypes, examples, and project-specific references. Tier 4 is history, vision, and simplified backlog. Tier 5 is FOMO and inactive inspiration. For non-obvious code work, Tier 1 should only carry the gate that asks concise business/product-direction and technical-implication questions before research; detailed profile lenses belong in Tier 2 or Tier 3. Full knowledgebase retagging must be a real audit pass, not a shallow sweep.

Rationale: Clear tier ownership prevents instruction bloat, keeps cacheable startup context stable, and makes routed knowledge loadable by task need instead of perceived importance.

Sources:
- `core/CONTEXT-TIERS.json` `tier_model` - Canonical Tier 1-5 context hierarchy.
- `core/CONTEXT-TIERS.json` `human_in_loop_research_gate` duplicate - Human-in-loop research input gate.
- `tools/knowledge/knowledge_index.py` `context_tier` duplicate - Compiled knowledge supports effective context tiers and tier filters.
name: engineering.workflow.tiered_context_hierarchy
summary: Keep always-loaded context small by classifying pipeline knowledge into five tiers before setting exact token budgets.
title: Tiered Context Hierarchy

compiled_content:
## engineering.workflow.verification_without_tests - Verification Discipline Without a Test Suite

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `best-practices`, `diagnostics`, `engineering`, `verification`, `workflow`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

When a project has no test runner (project_profile.test_coverage = 'none' or 'smoke-only'), substitute discipline: read all callers, trace execution, propose a manual smoke-test sequence, and explicitly state what was NOT verified and why.

When implementing changes in projects without an enforced test suite: (1) Read all callers of the symbols being modified before changing them - grep/find for usages, list them, ensure the change matches all caller expectations. (2) Trace execution through the change site: dispatch, workers, hooks, third-party APIs - surface any path the change might affect. (3) Prefer the smallest possible diff for risky changes; if a refactor would be cleaner, propose it as a separate task rather than bundling. (4) For each substantive change, propose a concrete manual smoke-test sequence the user (or QA) can run - exact endpoint/URL, parameters, expected vs actual outcome. (5) Explicitly state what was NOT verified and why ('BaRE billing path not exercised locally - verification deferred to QA on stage'). Do not claim end-to-end correctness from `php -l` or syntax-only checks. (6) When `php -l` / linter / inspection profile passes, say so by name - do not generalize that to 'verified'.

Rationale: Most legacy/maintenance-mode projects lack a green-build signal. Pretending otherwise produces false confidence. Substituting reading-callers + tracing + explicit unverified-list keeps the diagnosability bar high without inventing test infrastructure that is not there.

Sources:
- `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json` `verification_attached` duplicate - Verification-attached dimension supports manual reproduction steps when other verification is impractical.
name: engineering.workflow.verification_without_tests
summary: When a project has no test runner (project_profile.test_coverage = 'none' or 'smoke-only'), substitute discipline: read all callers, trace execution, propose a manual smoke-test sequence, and explicitly state what was NOT verified and why.
title: Verification Discipline Without a Test Suite

## engineering.workflow.web_research_spike_to_named_implementation_plan_gate - Web Research Spike To Named Implementation Plan Gate

- document: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`
- type: `rule`
- priority: `must`
- subtype: `workflow_rule`
- load_at_start: `true`
- context_tier: `1`
- tags: `engineering`, `workflow`, `architecture`, `coding`, `research`, `web-research`, `borrowed-before-invented`, `future-first`, `lightweight-futureproof`, `performance`, `diagnostics`, `planning`, `profile-fit`
- source_of_truth: `context-docs/ENGINEERING.json`

Non-obvious code-write work should research established archetypes and known failure modes before design, then name the implementation mechanism in the plan or execution note.

Before non-obvious source-code writes such as feature design, structural bugfixes, performance work, hitbox/runtime behavior, or architecture choices, run a lightweight web research spike unless the active plan already names a current research-backed mechanism. If the correct pattern depends on future behavior, ask concise scale/dynamism questions first: active/total counts, update frequency, batch size, data volume, dynamic variation, module/branch count, and expected growth order. Synthesize the result as a named implementation mechanism, then use local project patterns, DDD-ADIV, diagnostics, and verification to shape the implementation. Local patterns are an implementation lens, not an automatic veto; when research conflicts with local style, surface the conflict and ask.

Rationale: This keeps borrowed-before-invented concrete without bloating every task with heavy design docs. The gate catches known architecture, performance, diagnosability, and lifecycle traps early while preserving lightweight execution for obvious tiny fixes.

Sources:
- `core/AGENT-CONTRACT.json` `primary_known_solutions_directive` - Shared directive for web-first known-solution checks before non-obvious code writes.
- `project/code/PACK.json` `known_solution_bias` duplicate - Code pack applies the gate to generic programming work.
- `project/code/WORKFLOW.json` `general_workflow` duplicate - Executable code workflow sequence for the research spike and named mechanism.
- `FLOWS.json` `implementation` duplicate - Implementation flow names the gate before source edits.
name: engineering.workflow.web_research_spike_to_named_implementation_plan_gate
summary: Non-obvious code-write work should research established archetypes and known failure modes before design, then name the implementation mechanism in the plan or execution note.
title: Web Research Spike To Named Implementation Plan Gate
